<div><tool-call id="toolu_01NPUafJshP1vCJij3j2YccX"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"library(leaflet)\nlibrary(leaflet.extras)\nlibrary(dplyr)\nlibrary(htmlwidgets)\n\nmap_faciliti...[truncated, total 1954 chars]","intent":"Rebuild map using leaflet.extras addSearchFeatures"},"intent":"Rebuild map using leaflet.extras addSearchFeatures","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"library(leaflet)\nlibrary(leaflet.extras)\nlibrary(dplyr)\nlibrary(htmlwidgets)\n\nmap_facilities <- map_facilities |>\n  mutate(search_label = paste0(canonical_name, \" \\u2014 \", facility_city, \", \", facility_state))\n\nero_geocoded <- ero_geocoded |>\n  mutate(search_label = paste0(office_name, \" (ERO) \\u2014 \", city, \", \", state))\n\nm_search <- leaflet() |>\n  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) |>\n  addCircleMarkers(\n    data        = map_facilities,\n    lng = ~lon, lat = ~lat,\n    radius      = ~radius,\n    color       = ~type_pal(facility_type_wiki),\n    fillColor   = ~type_pal(facility_type_wiki),\n    fillOpacity = 0.6,\n    weight = 1, opacity = 0.9,\n    label  = ~search_label,\n    popup  = facility_popup,\n    group  = \"Official detention facilities\"\n  ) |>\n  addCircleMarkers(\n    data        = ero_geocoded,\n    lng = ~lon, lat = ~lat,\n    radius      = 9,\n    color |>\n  addSearchFeatures(\n    targetGroups = c(\"Official detention facilities\", \"ERO field offices\"),\n    options = searchFeaturesOptions(\n      propertyName      = \"label\",\n      zoom \"bottomleft\",\n    pal      = type_pal,\n    values   = map_facilities$facility_type_wiki,\n    title    = \"Detention facility type<br><small>(circle area \\u221d ADP)</small>\",\n    opacity  = 0.9\n  ) |>\n  addLegend(\n    position = \"bottomleft\",\n    colors   = \"red\",\n    labels   = \"ERO field office (holding room)\",\n    opacity  = 0.9\n  ) |>\n  addLayersControl(\n    overlayGroups = c(\"Official detention facilities\", \"ERO field offices\"),\n    options       = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE)\n  )\n\nm_search","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

## Databot



<div><thinking id="thinking-1773282259624-2l413">

A "closed" facility in our data is one where the `trajectory == "closed"` (present in FY19, absent from FY26). But actually, looking at the `facility_presence` data, we have `trajectory` values including "closed" and "transient" for facilities that are no longer active. The `map_facilities` dataframe already has a `trajectory` column.

The cleanest approach in leaflet is to put closed facilities in a separate group, then use `addLayersControl` to make that group toggleable and hidden by default.


Now I need to restructure the overlay groups so that open and closed detention facilities are separate. I'll split the facilities based on their trajectory status, then configure the layers control so that open facilities show by default while closed ones stay hidden. The cleanest leaflet approach is to split `map_facilities` into two groups and call `hideGroup()` on the closed one so it starts unchecked.

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01Fg77e8aBgU8ANraSzgGzz9"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"map_facilities |> count(trajectory)","intent":"Check trajectory distribution to define \"closed\""},"intent":"Check trajectory distribution to define \"closed\"","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"map_facilities |> count(trajectory)","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-result call-id="toolu_01Fg77e8aBgU8ANraSzgGzz9"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"status":"success","result":{"type":"content","value":[{"type":"text","text":"Output:\n# A tibble: 5 × 2\n  trajectory          n\n  <chr>           <int>\n1 closed        ...[truncated, total 217 chars]"}]},"resultPreview":{"type":"code","value":"# A tibble: 5 × 2\n  trajectory          n\n  <chr>           <int>\n1 closed             93\n2 continuous         78\n3 new               106\n4 persistent_gaps    40\n5 transient          31"},"elapsedMs":195}</script></tool-result></div>



<div><tool-call id="toolu_017ZqBW1Ei2tYEQzo9MfmqYC"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"library(leaflet)\nlibrary(leaflet.extras)\nlibrary(dplyr)\nlibrary(htmlwidgets)\n\nmap_open   <...[truncated, total 2741 chars]","intent":"Rebuild map with closed facilities as a separate hidden-by-default group"},"intent":"Rebuild map with closed facilities as a separate hidden-by-default group","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"library(leaflet)\nlibrary(leaflet.extras)\nlibrary(dplyr)\nlibrary(htmlwidgets)\n\nmap_open   <- map_facilities |> filter(!trajectory %in% c(\"closed\", \"transient\"))\nmap_closed <- map_facilities |> filter( trajectory %in% c(\"closed\", \"transient\"))\n\n# Separate popup vectors to match the filtered rows\nopen_popup   <- facility_popup[!map_facilities$trajectory %in% c(\"closed\", \"transient\")]\nclosed_popup <- facility_popup[ map_facilities$trajectory %in% c(\"closed\", \"transient\")]\n\nm_search <- leaflet() |>\n  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) |>\n  # Open facilities\n  addCircleMarkers(\n    data        = map_open,\n    lng = ~lon, lat = ~lat,\n    radius      = ~radius,\n    color       = ~type_pal(facility_type_wiki),\n    fillColor   = ~type_pal(facility_type_wiki),\n    fillOpacity = 0.6,\n    weight = 1, opacity = 0.9,\n    label  = ~search_label,\n    popup  = open_popup,\n    group  = \"Official detention facilities\"\n  ) |>\n  # Closed / transient facilities\n  addCircleMarkers(\n    data        = map_closed,\n    lng = ~lon, lat = ~lat,\n    radius      = ~radius,\n    color       = ~type_pal(facility_type_wiki),\n    fillColor   = ~type_pal(facility_type_wiki),\n    fillOpacity = 0.25,\n    weight = 1, opacity = 0.5,\n    label  = ~search_label,\n    popup  = closed_popup,\n    group  = \"Closed / transient facilities\"\n  ) |>\n  # ERO field offices\n  addCircleMarkers(\n    data        = ero_geocoded,\n    lng = ~lon, lat = ~lat,\n    radius      = 9,\n    color       = \"black\",\n    fillColor   = \"#ff0000\",\n    fillOpacity = 0.85,\n    weight = 2, opacity = 1,\n    label  = ~search_label,\n    popup  = ero_popup,\n    group  = \"ERO field offices\"\n  ) |>\n  addSearchFeatures(\n    targetGroups = c(\"Official detention facilities\",\n                     \"Closed / transient facilities\",\n                     \"ERO field offices\"),\n    options = searchFeaturesOptions(\n      propertyName         = \"label\",\n      zoom                 = 12,\n      openPopup            = TRUE,\n      firstTipSubmit       = TRUE,\n      autoCollapse         = TRUE,\n      hideMarkerOnCollapse = TRUE\n    )\n  ) |>\n  addLegend(\n    position = \"bottomleft\",\n    pal      = type_pal,\n    values   = map_facilities$facility_type_wiki,\n    title    = \"Detention facility type<br><small>(circle area \\u221d ADP)</small>\",\n    opacity  = 0.9\n  ) |>\n  addLegend(\n    position = \"bottomleft\",\n    colors   = \"red\",\n    labels   = \"ERO field office (holding room)\",\n    opacity  = 0.9\n  ) |>\n  addLayersControl(\n    overlayGroups = c(\"Official detention facilities\",\n                      \"Closed / transient facilities\",\n                      \"ERO field offices\"),\n    options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE)\n  ) |>\n  hideGroup(\"Closed / transient facilities\")\n\nm_search","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-result call-id="toolu_017ZqBW1Ei2tYEQzo9MfmqYC"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"status":"success","result":{"type":"content","value":[{"type":"text","text":"Execution time: 551ms"}]},"resultPreview":{"type":"code","value":""},"elapsedMs":640}</script></tool-result></div>



<div><tool-call id="toolu_012V3UpSThpiMMbY1wBuhnUd"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"saveWidget(m_search, here::here(\"data/maps/ero-detention-network.html\"), selfcontained = TRUE)\nmessage(\"Saved.\")","intent":"Save updated map"},"intent":"Save updated map","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"saveWidget(m_search, here::here(\"data/maps/ero-detention-network.html\"), selfcontained = TRUE)\nmessage(\"Saved.\")","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-result call-id="toolu_012V3UpSThpiMMbY1wBuhnUd"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"status":"success","result":{"type":"content","value":[{"type":"text","text":"Output:\nSaved.\n\n\nExecution time: 4890ms"}]},"resultPreview":{"type":"code","value":"Saved."},"elapsedMs":5000}</script></tool-result></div>

Done. A few implementation notes:

- **"Closed"** is defined as `trajectory %in% c("closed", "transient")` — 124 facilities absent from FY26. The remaining 224 (continuous, new, persistent_gaps) are in the default-on group.
- Closed facilities use reduced `fillOpacity = 0.25` / `opacity = 0.5` so they read as faded if you do turn the layer on, visually distinguishing them from active facilities without a separate color scheme.
- The search covers all three groups, so closed facilities are still findable by name even when the layer is hidden — leaflet will fly to the location and reveal the marker when selected.

I'm setting the map center to roughly the middle of the continental US and using zoom level 4, which with my resolution scale gives good coverage of the entire country. I need to finalize the resolution array and zoom constraints, though I'm still weighing whether the custom Albers CRS approach is worth the complexity versus just constraining a standard map.

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01P9RkymXHBY98bVLComAD4A"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"# Check for Alaska/Hawaii facilities that might need special handling\nmap_facilities |>\n  ...[truncated, total 281 chars]","intent":"Check for non-contiguous US facilities and maps package availability"},"intent":"Check for non-contiguous US facilities and maps package availability","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"# Check for Alaska/Hawaii facilities that might need special handling\nmap_facilities |>\n  filter(facility_state %in% c(\"AK\", \"HI\", \"PR\", \"VI\")) |>\n  select(canonical_name, facility_state, lon, lat)\n\n# Also check if maps package is available\nrequireNamespace(\"maps\", quietly = TRUE)","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

Actually, let me think about this differently. The user asked "Can we switch to a different projection?" - I should give an honest technical answer: Yes, but with a key limitation. Leaflet.js uses Web Mercator internally for tile layers, and while custom projections via proj4leaflet are technically supported, standard tile servers like CartoDB and OpenStreetMap won't render correctly in non-Mercator projections—I'd need to replace them with polygon-based backgrounds instead.

Two Pacific facilities (Saipan/MP and Guam/GU) to exclude, and one geocoding anomaly — "San Juan Staging" landed at -107°, 38° (Colorado) rather than Puerto Rico. Worth flagging.

On projection: Leaflet's tile servers only serve Web Mercator, so switching CRS means replacing the CartoDB basemap with polygon state outlines. That gives a clean cartographic look but no roads or labels. I'll implement it that way since you asked for a proper projection.

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01N2EyzBNFvx2nvukMkoKW96"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"library(leaflet)\nlibrary(leaflet.extras)\nlibrary(sf)\nlibrary(dplyr)\nlibrary(htmlwidgets)\n\n...[truncated, total 828 chars]","intent":"Filter out Pacific territory facilities and misgeocoded San Juan Staging"},"intent":"Filter out Pacific territory facilities and misgeocoded San Juan Staging","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"library(leaflet)\nlibrary(leaflet.extras)\nlibrary(sf)\nlibrary(dplyr)\nlibrary(htmlwidgets)\n\n# ── Exclusions ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n# Exclude Pacific territories (lon > 0) and the misgeocoded San Juan Staging\nexclude_ids <- map_facilities |>\n  filter(facility_state %in% c(\"MP\", \"GU\") |\n           (canonical_name == \"San Juan Staging\")) |>\n  pull(canonical_id)\n\nmap_open_us   <- map_open   |> filter(!canonical_id %in% exclude_ids)\nmap_closed_us <- map_closed |> filter(!canonical_id %in% exclude_ids)\n\nopen_popup_us   <- open_popup[  !map_open$canonical_id   %in% exclude_ids]\nclosed_popup_us <- closed_popup[!map_closed$canonical_id %in% exclude_ids]\n\ncat(\"Excluded:\", length(exclude_ids), \"facilities\\n\")\ncat(\"Remaining open:\", nrow(map_open_us), \" closed:\", nrow(map_closed_us), \"\\n\")","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-result call-id="toolu_01N2EyzBNFvx2nvukMkoKW96"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"status":"success","result":{"type":"content","value":[{"type":"text","text":"Output:\nExcluded: 3 facilities\nRemaining open: 221  closed: 124 \n\n\nExecution time: 325ms"}]},"resultPreview":{"type":"code","value":"Excluded: 3 facilities\nRemaining open: 221  closed: 124"},"elapsedMs":403}</script></tool-result></div>



<div><tool-call id="toolu_01QeJEkYkHcNYq2vqtBtA3gv"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"# ── Background: US state + territory polygons ─────────────────────────────────\nstates_sf...[truncated, total 3403 chars]","intent":"Build Albers Equal Area map with state polygon background, excluded Pacific facilities, panning constraints"},"intent":"Build Albers Equal Area map with state polygon background, excluded Pacific facilities, panning constraints","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"# ── Background: US state + territory polygons ─────────────────────────────────\nstates_sf <- sf::st_as_sf(\n  maps::map(\"state\", fill = TRUE, plot = FALSE)\n)\n# Add Alaska, Hawaii, Puerto Rico from world dataset for completeness\nak_hi_pr <- sf::st_as_sf(\n  maps::map(\"world\",\n            regions = c(\"USA:Alaska\", \"USA:Hawaii\", \"Puerto Rico\"),\n            fill = TRUE, plot = FALSE)\n)\nbackground_sf <- bind_rows(states_sf, ak_hi_pr)\n\n# ── Albers Equal Area CRS (ESRI:102003) ───────────────────────────────────────\nalbers <- leafletCRS(\n  crsClass = \"L.Proj.CRS\",\n  code     = \"ESRI:102003\",\n  proj4def = paste(\"+proj=aea +lat_1=29.5 +lat_2=45.5 +lat_0=37.5\",\n                   \"+lon_0=-96 +x_0=0 +y_0=0\",\n                   \"+ellps=GRS80 +datum=NAD83 +units=m +no_defs\"),\n  resolutions = 2^(16:6)   # zoom levels 0–10, from 65536 to 64 m/px\n)\n\n# ── Map ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\nm_albers <- leaflet(options = leafletOptions(crs = albers)) |>\n  addPolygons(\n    data        = background_sf,\n    fillColor   = \"#e8e8e8\",\n    fillOpacity = 1,\n    color       = \"white\",\n    weight      = 0.8,\n    smoothFactor = 0.5\n  ) |>\n  addCircleMarkers(\n    data        = map_open_us,\n    lng = ~lon, lat = ~lat,\n    radius      = ~radius,\n    color       = ~type_pal(facility_type_wiki),\n    fillColor   = ~type_pal(facility_type_wiki),\n    fillOpacity = 0.7,\n    weight = 1, opacity = 0.9,\n    label  = ~search_label,\n    popup  = open_popup_us,\n    group  = \"Official detention facilities\"\n  ) |>\n  addCircleMarkers(\n    data        = map_closed_us,\n    lng = ~lon, lat = ~lat,\n    radius      = ~radius,\n    color       = ~type_pal(facility_type_wiki),\n    fillColor   = ~type_pal(facility_type_wiki),\n    fillOpacity = 0.25,\n    weight = 1, opacity = 0.5,\n    label  = ~search_label,\n    popup  = closed_popup_us,\n    group  = \"Closed / transient facilities\"\n  ) |>\n  addCircleMarkers(\n    data        = ero_geocoded,\n    lng = ~lon, lat = ~lat,\n    radius      = 9,\n    color       = \"black\",\n    fillColor   = \"#ff0000\",\n    fillOpacity = 0.85,\n    weight = 2, opacity = 1,\n    label  = ~search_label,\n    popup  = ero_popup,\n    group  = \"ERO field offices\"\n  ) |>\n  addSearchFeatures(\n    targetGroups = c(\"Official detention facilities\",\n                     \"Closed / transient facilities\",\n                     \"ERO field offices\"),\n    options = searchFeaturesOptions(\n      propertyName         = \"label\",\n      zoom                 = 7,\n      openPopup            = TRUE,\n      firstTipSubmit       = TRUE,\n      autoCollapse         = TRUE,\n      hideMarkerOnCollapse = TRUE\n    )\n  ) |>\n  addLegend(\n    position = \"bottomleft\",\n    pal      = type_pal,\n    values   = map_facilities$facility_type_wiki,\n    title    = \"Detention facility type<br><small>(circle area \\u221d ADP)</small>\",\n    opacity  = 0.9\n  ) |>\n  addLegend(\n    position = \"bottomleft\",\n    colors   = \"red\",\n    labels   = \"ERO field office (holding room)\",\n    opacity  = 0.9\n  ) |>\n  addLayersControl(\n    overlayGroups = c(\"Official detention facilities\",\n                      \"Closed / transient facilities\",\n                      \"ERO field offices\"),\n    options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE)\n  ) |>\n  hideGroup(\"Closed / transient facilities\") |>\n  setView(lng = -96, lat = 38, zoom = 4) |>\n  setMaxBounds(lng1 = -175, lat1 = 15, lng2 = -60, lat2 = 73)\n\nm_albers","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01VtaQDk5gycEWotYno1SVMv"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"states_sf <- sf::st_read(\n  \"https://raw.githubusercontent.com/PublicaMundi/MappingAPI/mas...[truncated, total 274 chars]","intent":"Fetch US states GeoJSON directly via sf::st_read"},"intent":"Fetch US states GeoJSON directly via sf::st_read","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"states_sf <- sf::st_read(\n  \"https://raw.githubusercontent.com/PublicaMundi/MappingAPI/master/data/geojson/us-states.json\",\n  quiet = TRUE\n)\ncat(nrow(states_sf), \"features,\", ncol(states_sf), \"columns\\n\")\ncat(\"CRS:\", sf::st_crs(states_sf)$epsg, \"\\n\")\nhead(states_sf[\"name\"])","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

Done. A few notes on what changed and what was traded off:

**Changes:**
- Saipan (MP) and Guam (GU) excluded (3 facilities total — the two Pacific territories plus the misgeocoded San Juan Staging, which was landing in Colorado)
- Projection: **Albers Equal Area Conic** (ESRI:102003), standard for US thematic mapping — states in the northeast no longer appear bloated relative to southern states
- Panning constrained to roughly 15°–73°N, 175°W–60°W (`maxBoundsViscosity = 1.0` makes the boundary hard rather than springy), zoom locked to levels 3–9

**The tradeoff:** the CartoDB basemap is gone — it only tiles in Web Mercator. The state polygon layer (fetched from PublicaMundi's GeoJSON via `sf::st_read`) serves as the background instead. The `interactive = FALSE` path option means hovering over state polygons doesn't trigger any tooltip.

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01VNwA4qarRQYnYXqpsxa2dB"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"saveWidget(m_search, here::here(\"data/maps/ero-detention-network.html\"), selfcontained = TRUE)\nmessage(\"Restored.\")","intent":"Overwrite the Albers map file with the previous m_search version"},"intent":"Overwrite the Albers map file with the previous m_search version","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"saveWidget(m_search, here::here(\"data/maps/ero-detention-network.html\"), selfcontained = TRUE)\nmessage(\"Restored.\")","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-result call-id="toolu_01VNwA4qarRQYnYXqpsxa2dB"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"status":"success","result":{"type":"content","value":[{"type":"text","text":"Output:\nRestored.\n\n\nExecution time: 4606ms"}]},"resultPreview":{"type":"code","value":"Restored."},"elapsedMs":4673}</script></tool-result></div>

Restored. The map is back to the CartoDB Positron basemap with ADP-scaled circles, `leaflet.extras` search, and the closed facilities checkbox off by default.

For the record on the projection question: Leaflet's standard tile servers (CartoDB, OpenStreetMap, etc.) only serve tiles in Web Mercator (EPSG:3857). Switching to a US-appropriate projection like Albers Equal Area means the tile layer breaks entirely — you'd have to replace it with static polygon outlines, which loses roads, labels, and the ability to zoom into street level. The distortion from Mercator is noticeable at high latitudes (Alaska reads as oversized) but fairly modest across the contiguous US latitude range (~25°–50°N), where it amounts to roughly a 10–15% vertical stretch. For a thematic overview map, it's generally an acceptable tradeoff to keep the interactive tile basemap.

Neither `arrow` nor `feather` is installed. You'll need one to read the file. The `arrow` package is the current standard:

```r
install.packages("arrow")
```

Or the lighter-weight original:

```r
install.packages("feather")
```

`arrow` is preferred — it's faster, actively maintained, and the same package you'd use if the data were later updated to Parquet format. Once installed, come back and I'll load the file and walk through the structure against the documentation.

In the meantime, here's what the documentation tells us about what to expect. The dataset is constructed at the **facility-day level** from ICE's individual-level detentions data, where facilities are identified by a **detention facility code** field. Each row represents one facility on one date, with counts of:

- Total individuals detained at any point during the day
- Total detained overnight (at midnight)
- Subgroup breakdowns: by **gender** (ICE-reported), **age** (under 18 by birth year), and **prior criminal conviction** (ICE-recorded)

The key linking field for our purposes will be that **detention facility code** — we'll need to understand how it maps to the `canonical_name`/`canonical_id` system in our panel data. That crosswalk is the first task.

<div><tool-call id="toolu_014SeHKKuQYS5DZrcuHkF3aT"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"# How many codes/names appear more than once (code→name isn't 1:1?)\ndp |>\n  distinct(deten...[truncated, total 566 chars]","intent":"Check code→name mapping consistency and sample non-zero rows"},"intent":"Check code→name mapping consistency and sample non-zero rows","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"# How many codes/names appear more than once (code→name isn't 1:1?)\ndp |>\n  distinct(detention_facility_code, detention_facility) |>\n  add_count(detention_facility_code, name = \"names_per_code\") |>\n  filter(names_per_code > 1) |>\n  arrange(detention_facility_code)\n\n# And a sample of non-zero rows to see typical values\ndp |>\n  filter(n_detained > 0) |>\n  select(detention_facility_code, detention_facility, state, date,\n         n_detained, n_detained_at_midnight, n_detained_male,\n         n_detained_female, n_detained_convicted_criminal) |>\n  slice_sample(n = 6)","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><thinking id="thinking-1773285959070-5jbvk">
Now I have a good picture of the data. Let me summarize what I've found:1. **661,928 rows** × **12 columns** — facility-day level data
2. **Date range**: 2023-09-01 to 2025-10-15
3. **853 distinct facility codes**, **841 distinct facility names** — the codes are unique identifiers, no code maps to multiple names
4. **Source state**: The data comes from multiple ICE data releases (different `source_state` / `date_state` values), meaning this is a merged dataset across multiple FOIA releases. The `ddp-manual` source (166,840 rows) is a manually compiled part of the dataset.


5. The dataset includes facility identifiers (codes and names), location information by state, daily dates, and several detention counts — including the total number of individuals detained during each day and those present at midnight, plus additional demographic breakdowns.

6. I'm noticing that `source_state` contains numeric strings that appear to be either years or Excel date serial numbers — "2017" likely represents a 2017 data release, while values like "41855" convert to dates around mid-2014, though the `date_state` field shows 2024-01-01, which creates some inconsistency I need to investigate further.

7. The data sources break down into older releases from 2017, manually compiled DDP data, and 776 rows with missing source information, with dates ranging from 2017 through 2026. The critical linking point is `detention_facility_code`, which identifies individual detention facilities in ICE's data — I need to determine whether this code exists in the facilities panel or if I'll need to match records by facility name and state instead.

The facility names in the ICE data are all-caps like "SW VIRGINIA REG JAIL AUTH-ABG FAC" while the spreadsheet uses title case like "Southwest Virginia Regional Jail Authority - Abingdon Facility", so fuzzy matching would be necessary. The codes like "ABIRJVA" and "BTV" appear to be ICE's internal identifiers, and I'm not certain our spreadsheet data includes a corresponding code field. I should check whether the facilities panel or any crosswalk contains a code column that would allow a direct join, otherwise I'll need to rely on fuzzy name and state matching.

Looking at the actual variable names in our data—`facility_name`, `facility_city`, `facility_state`, `facility_zip`, `facility_address`—there's no explicit facility code field. But examining the codes more closely, I notice patterns like "GJCHOLD" and "DENHOLD" with the "HOLD" suffix, which suggests these are ERO field office holding rooms rather than official detention facilities. So the 853 distinct facility codes in the DDP data likely include both official ICE detention facilities and these temporary holding rooms.

This means my linking strategy needs to account for two categories: matching official facilities to our canonical system, and separately flagging the holding rooms as new data not in our existing spreadsheets.
</thinking></div>



<div><tool-call id="toolu_01BNJ6CSf6HHxEznQF3zfgrN"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"# How many facilities have zero detention throughout? vs non-zero?\ndp |>\n  group_by(detent...[truncated, total 497 chars]","intent":"Distinguish holding rooms from official detention facilities in the dataset"},"intent":"Distinguish holding rooms from official detention facilities in the dataset","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"# How many facilities have zero detention throughout? vs non-zero?\ndp |>\n  group_by(detention_facility_code, detention_facility, state) |>\n  summarise(max_detained = max(n_detained), .groups = \"drop\") |>\n  count(ever_used = max_detained > 0)\n\n# Do any codes look like ERO holding rooms?\ndp |>\n  distinct(detention_facility_code, detention_facility, state) |>\n  filter(grepl(\"HOLD|HOLDROOM|HOLDING|ERO|FIELD\", detention_facility,\n               ignore.case = TRUE)) |>\n  arrange(detention_facility)","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

HOLD RM          GA   \n 9 AUSHOLD                 AUSTIN DRO HOLD ROOM           TX   \n10 BKLHOLD                 BAKERFIELD HOLD                CA   \n# ℹ 159 more rows\n# ℹ Use `print(n = ...)` to see more rows"},"elapsedMs":296}</script></tool-result></div>

Here's what the data contains:

**Structure:** 661,928 rows covering **2023-09-01 to 2025-10-15**, one row per facility-day. The 12 columns are:

| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| `detention_facility_code` | Short ICE internal code (e.g. `"ATLHOLD"`, `"BTV"`, `"PIC"`) — the primary key |
| `detention_facility` | All-caps facility name |
| `state` | 2-letter state |
| `date` | Date of observation |
| `n_detained` | People detained at any point that day |
| `n_detained_at_midnight` | People held overnight |
| `n_detained_male/female` | Gender breakdown |
| `n_detained_convicted_criminal` | With ICE-recorded prior conviction |
| `n_detained_possibly_under_18` | Age flag (based on birth year) |
| `source_state` / `date_state` | Provenance: which ICE data release the row came from |

**853 distinct facility codes, all of which show non-zero population at some point.** Crucially, a large share of those 853 are **holding rooms** — 169 entries match names containing "HOLD", "HOLDROOM", etc.: `ATLHOLD` = "ATLANTA DIST. HOLD RM", `DENHOLD` = "DENVER HOLD ROOM", and so on. This dataset is the primary evidence base for the Guardian's analysis and directly captures the informal detention infrastructure we've been mapping.

**For the linking task**, the `detention_facility_code` has no counterpart in our ICE spreadsheet data — we'll need to match by name + state. The holding room entries will need to be handled separately: most won't match anything in `facilities_panel` (they're not in the official spreadsheets), but they should match against our `ero_field_offices` data where possible.

I'll suggest some questions below.


<span class="suggestion">Build the crosswalk: fuzzy-match `detention_facility` + `state` against `canonical_name` + `facility_state` in our panel, then separately match holding-room codes against the ERO field offices.</span>


<span class="suggestion">Before matching, filter out the zero-population rows and look at the date coverage per facility — the `source_state` provenance field suggests this is a splice of several FOIA releases with different coverage windows.</span>


<span class="suggestion">Add `detention_facility_code` as a stored field in the pipeline once the crosswalk is built, so the DDP daily population data can be joined to canonical facilities without re-matching.</span>






## User

So clearly, HOLD is a meaningful suffix in the detention_facility_code. Is there any other structure to the codes used? What major types of do we see and with what frequency?

For each detention_facility calculate the maximum, minimum, and average n_detained.



## Databot

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01KSS87XFrv1GTX5mKSpjxD8"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"library(dplyr)\nlibrary(stringr)\n\n# ── Code structure ─────────────────────────────────────...[truncated, total 869 chars]","intent":"Analyze detention_facility_code length and suffix patterns"},"intent":"Analyze detention_facility_code length and suffix patterns","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"library(dplyr)\nlibrary(stringr)\n\n# ── Code structure ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\ncodes <- dp |> distinct(detention_facility_code, detention_facility, state)\n\n# Length distribution\ncat(\"── Code lengths ──\\n\")\ncodes |> count(len = nchar(detention_facility_code)) |> print()\n\n# Known meaningful suffixes from facility names\ncat(\"\\n── Suffix patterns ──\\n\")\ncodes |>\n  mutate(suffix = case_when(\n    str_ends(detention_facility_code, \"HOLD\") ~ \"HOLD\",\n    str_ends(detention_facility_code, \"SPC\")  ~ \"SPC\",\n    str_ends(detention_facility_code, \"CDF\")  ~ \"CDF\",\n    str_ends(detention_facility_code, \"BOP\")  ~ \"BOP\",\n    str_ends(detention_facility_code, \"FRC\")  ~ \"FRC\",\n    str_ends(detention_facility_code, \"ICA\")  ~ \"ICA\",\n    TRUE                                       ~ \"(none)\"\n  )) |>\n  count(suffix, sort = TRUE) |>\n  print(n = 20)","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-result call-id="toolu_01KSS87XFrv1GTX5mKSpjxD8"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"status":"success","result":{"type":"content","value":[{"type":"text","text":"Output:\n── Code lengths ──\n# A tibble: 3 × 2\n    len     n\n  <int> <int>\n1     3    14\n2  ...[truncated, total 254 chars]"}]},"resultPreview":{"type":"code","value":"── Code lengths ──\n# A tibble: 3 × 2\n    len     n\n  <int> <int>\n1     3    14\n2     6    25\n3     7   814\n\n── Suffix patterns ──\n# A tibble: 3 × 2\n  suffix     n\n  <chr>  <int>\n1 (none)   678\n2 HOLD     171\n3 CDF        4"},"elapsedMs":452}</script></tool-result></div>

3. **7-character codes** break down into three groups: those ending in "HOLD" (171 codes) are holding rooms at ERO field offices, those ending in "CDF" (4 codes) are Contract Detention Facilities, and the remaining 678 codes appear to be IGSAs, jails, and other contracted facilities structured with a location prefix and state abbreviation suffix. <div><tool-call id="toolu_01VoGTCtoLt8K9L8fpLyWrAe"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"# Other type-indicating prefixes in 7-char codes\ncat(\"── Prefixes in 7-char codes ──\\n\")\nc...[truncated, total 545 chars]","intent":"Look for other prefix patterns in the 7-char codes"},"intent":"Look for other prefix patterns in the 7-char codes","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"# Other type-indicating prefixes in 7-char codes\ncat(\"── Prefixes in 7-char codes ──\\n\")\ncodes |>\n  filter(nchar(detention_facility_code) == 7) |>\n  mutate(prefix3 = str_sub(detention_facility_code, 1, 3)) |>\n  count(prefix3, sort = TRUE) |>\n  filter(n >= 3) |>\n  left_join(\n    codes |>\n      filter(nchar(detention_facility_code) == 7) |>\n      mutate(prefix3 = str_sub(detention_facility_code, 1, 3)) |>\n      group_by(prefix3) |>\n      slice_head(n = 1) |>\n      select(prefix3, detention_facility),\n    by = \"prefix3\"\n  ) |>\n  print(n = 20)","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01Kj7BJ6u73yA3yDFMLueiGK"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"# Per-facility summary stats (non-zero days only for meaningful min)\nfacility_stats <- dp ...[truncated, total 702 chars]","intent":"Calculate per-facility max, min, and average n_detained (on non-zero days)"},"intent":"Calculate per-facility max, min, and average n_detained (on non-zero days)","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"# Per-facility summary stats (non-zero days only for meaningful min)\nfacility_stats <- dp |>\n  group_by(detention_facility_code, detention_facility, state) |>\n  summarise(\n    days_observed   = n(),\n    days_with_pop   = sum(n_detained > 0),\n    max_detained    = max(n_detained),\n    min_detained    = min(n_detained[n_detained > 0], default = NA_integer_),\n    avg_detained    = round(mean(n_detained[n_detained > 0], na.rm = TRUE), 1),\n    .groups = \"drop\"\n  ) |>\n  arrange(desc(max_detained))\n\ncat(\"── Top 20 by peak population ──\\n\")\nfacility_stats |>\n  select(detention_facility_code, detention_facility, state,\n         max_detained, min_detained, avg_detained, days_with_pop) |>\n  print(n = 20)","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><thinking id="thinking-1773286272412-obkdh">
Interesting - `min_detained` is NA for all the top facilities. That's because I used `min(n_detained[n_detained > 0], default = NA_integer_)` but this appears to be returning NA. Let me check... actually, the issue might be that these facilities never had a non-zero population? No wait, we established that all 853 facilities have non-zero population at some point. The problem is that these facilities do have zero-population days too (perhaps they were operational only during part of the date range). The min on non-zero days should work... 

Oh wait, the issue is the `default = NA_integer_` argument. 

Actually, I think the problem is that when I filter with `n_detained[n_detained > 0]`, if all values end up being NA or zero, the resulting vector is empty, and `min()` on an empty vector returns Inf with a warning - but I'm seeing NA instead, which suggests the subsetting condition itself might not be working as expected. The fact that ALL top 20 facilities have `min_detained = NA` is suspicious and points to something systematically wrong with how the condition is being evaluated.

Wait, I just realized - base R's `min()` function doesn't actually have a `default` argument at all! That's a `purrr` function feature. So when I'm passing `default = NA_integer_` to `min()`, it's not being treated as a default for empty vectors - it's being passed as an actual value to minimize, which means `min(c(some_values, NA_integer_))` returns NA because `na.rm` defaults to FALSE. I need to use a conditional check instead to handle the empty case properly. I see the issue now — `default` isn't actually a valid argument for `min()`, so passing `NA_integer_` there just gets treated as an extra value, which causes the whole result to become NA. Since all facilities have at least one detention day, I can simply use `min(n_detained[n_detained > 0])` without any default argument, and this will fix the bug affecting even the top facilities in the results.

Before I show the corrected stats, let me clarify the code structure I've identified: the 3-character facility codes represent ICE Service Processing Centers and Staging Facilities that are directly operated by ICE, while the 6-character codes are mostly Bureau of Prisons facilities (prefixed with `BOP`) that house ICE detainees, with a couple of exceptions like Delaney Hall in New Jersey.

The bulk of the dataset consists of 7-character codes—mostly IGSAs (county jails and private facilities contracted by ICE)—which follow a location-plus-state pattern like `STWRTGA` for Stewart, Georgia. There are also smaller groups with `HOLD` suffixes representing ERO field office holding rooms and `CDF` suffixes for Contract Detention Facilities.

Now let me recalculate the statistics with the correct minimum value.
</thinking></div>

The `min()` NAs are a bug — base R's `min()` has no `default` argument, so `default = NA_integer_` was being passed as a value. Let me fix that.

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01GiQrZRKQub9mGQHFbkp21u"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"facility_stats <- dp |>\n  group_by(detention_facility_code, detention_facility, state) |>\n...[truncated, total 545 chars]","intent":"Recompute per-facility stats with corrected min calculation"},"intent":"Recompute per-facility stats with corrected min calculation","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"facility_stats <- dp |>\n  group_by(detention_facility_code, detention_facility, state) |>\n  summarise(\n    days_observed = n(),\n    days_with_pop = sum(n_detained > 0),\n    max_detained  = max(n_detained),\n    min_detained  = min(n_detained[n_detained > 0]),\n    avg_detained  = round(mean(n_detained[n_detained > 0]), 1),\n    .groups = \"drop\"\n  ) |>\n  arrange(desc(max_detained))\n\nfacility_stats |>\n  select(detention_facility_code, detention_facility, state,\n         max_detained, min_detained, avg_detained, days_with_pop) |>\n  print(n = 20)","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

        1126        1418.\n13 EPSSFTX                 EL PASO SOFT SIDED FACILITY  TX            1606            1         432.\n14 CSCNWWA                 NW ICE PROCESSING CTR        WA            1604          507         928.\n15 FLDSSFS                 FLORIDA SOFT-SIDED FACILITY… FL            1482            1         638.\n16 MTGPCTX                 MONTGOMERY PROCESSING CTR    TX            1460          792        1192.\n17 JENADLA                 CENTRAL LOUISIANA ICE PROC … LA            1422          816        1144.\n18 KRNRCTX                 KARNES CO IMMIGRATION PROCE… TX The code structure is now clear. Three tiers:

**3-character codes (14 facilities)** — ICE's own Service Processing Centers and Staging Facilities: `BTV`, `PIC`, `KRO`, `EPC`, etc. These are the most directly ICE-operated facilities.

**6-character codes (25 facilities)** — Almost entirely `BOP` + 3-letter location: federal Bureau of Prisons facilities housing ICE detainees (`BOPBRO` = Brooklyn MDC, `BOPGUA` = Guaynabo, etc.). Two exceptions: `DHDFNJ` (Delaney Hall) and `EROFCB` (ERO El Paso Camp East Montana), which appear to have been assigned 6-char codes for other reasons.

**7-character codes (814 facilities)** — The bulk of the dataset, with two identifiable suffixes:
- **`HOLD` (171)**: ERO field office holding rooms — the informal detention infrastructure
- **`CDF` (4)**: Contract Detention Facilities (`DENICDF`, `ELZICDF`, `HOUICDF`, `LRDICDF`)
- **No suffix (639)**: IGSAs and other contracted jails/facilities, structured as `[location abbreviation][state]` — e.g., `STWRTGA` = Stewart/GA, `ADAMSMS` = Adams County/MS, `ADLNTCA` = Adelanto/CA

On the per-facility stats: `EROFCB` (ERO El Paso Camp East Montana) tops the list at a max of 3,116 — a striking number for what is formally classified as an ERO-operated site rather than an official detention facility. Several facilities show very low minimums (1–2) suggesting they were ramping up or winding down during the observation window.

I'll suggest some questions below.


<span class="suggestion">Use the `[location][state]` structure of the 7-char codes to bootstrap a first-pass join to `canonical_facilities` by state, then fuzzy-match on the location prefix against facility names.</span>


<span class="suggestion">Separate the 171 HOLD-suffix codes into their own table and attempt to match them against `ero_field_offices` by state and city abbreviation in the prefix.</span>

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01VgpRDZALpEFZ8XpjhRUWWC"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"library(dplyr)\nlibrary(stringr)\n\n# ── Q1: Systematic suffix scan on 7-char codes ─────────...[truncated, total 974 chars]","intent":"Scan all suffix lengths 2-4 in 7-char codes for meaningful patterns"},"intent":"Scan all suffix lengths 2-4 in 7-char codes for meaningful patterns","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"library(dplyr)\nlibrary(stringr)\n\n# ── Q1: Systematic suffix scan on 7-char codes ───────────────────────────────\nseven_char <- codes |> filter(nchar(detention_facility_code) == 7)\n\n# Pull every possible suffix length 2, 3, 4 and count\nbind_rows(\n  seven_char |> mutate(suffix = str_sub(detention_facility_code, -2), len = 2L),\n  seven_char |> mutate(suffix = str_sub(detention_facility_code, -3), len = 3L),\n  seven_char |> mutate(suffix = str_sub(detention_facility_code, -4), len = 4L)\n) |>\n  count(len, suffix, sort = FALSE) |>\n  filter(n >= 4) |>\n  arrange(len, desc(n)) |>\n  left_join(\n    bind_rows(\n      seven_char |> mutate(suffix = str_sub(detention_facility_code, -2)),\n      seven_char |> mutate(suffix = str_sub(detention_facility_code, -3)),\n      seven_char |> mutate(suffix = str_sub(detention_facility_code, -4))\n    ) |>\n      group_by(suffix) |>\n      slice_head(n = 1) |>\n      select(suffix, detention_facility),\n    by = \"suffix\"\n  ) |>\n  print(n = 50)","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01Gh8WDit7UdfUhxXjtCn8q4"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"# Finish the suffix scan - 3 and 4 char, looking past the state abbrev pattern\n# Focus on ...[truncated, total 1057 chars]","intent":"Find 3-char suffixes that aren't just location+state encodings"},"intent":"Find 3-char suffixes that aren't just location+state encodings","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"# Finish the suffix scan - 3 and 4 char, looking past the state abbrev pattern\n# Focus on non-state-abbreviation 3-char suffixes\nus_states_abbr <- c(\"AL\",\"AK\",\"AZ\",\"AR\",\"CA\",\"CO\",\"CT\",\"DE\",\"FL\",\"GA\",\"HI\",\"ID\",\n                    \"IL\",\"IN\",\"IA\",\"KS\",\"KY\",\"LA\",\"ME\",\"MD\",\"MA\",\"MI\",\"MN\",\"MS\",\n                    \"MO\",\"MT\",\"NE\",\"NV\",\"NH\",\"NJ\",\"NM\",\"NY\",\"NC\",\"ND\",\"OH\",\"OK\",\n                    \"OR\",\"PA\",\"RI\",\"SC\",\"SD\",\"TN\",\"TX\",\"UT\",\"VT\",\"VA\",\"WA\",\"WV\",\n                    \"WI\",\"WY\",\"DC\",\"PR\",\"GU\",\"VI\",\"MP\")\n\ncat(\"── 3-char suffixes that are NOT [X]+state ──\\n\")\nseven_char |>\n  mutate(\n    suffix2 = str_sub(detention_facility_code, -2),\n    suffix3 = str_sub(detention_facility_code, -3)\n  ) |>\n  filter(!suffix2 %in% us_states_abbr) |>   # exclude plain state suffix\n  count(suffix3, sort = TRUE) |>\n  filter(n >= 3) |>\n  left_join(\n    seven_char |>\n      mutate(suffix3 = str_sub(detention_facility_code, -3)) |>\n      group_by(suffix3) |>\n      slice_head(n = 1) |>\n      select(suffix3, detention_facility),\n    by = \"suffix3\"\n  ) |>\n  print(n = 30)","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-result call-id="toolu_01Gh8WDit7UdfUhxXjtCn8q4"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"status":"success","result":{"type":"content","value":[{"type":"text","text":"Output:\n── 3-char suffixes that are NOT [X]+state ──\n# A tibble: 2 × 3\n  suffix3     n det...[truncated, total 298 chars]"}]},"resultPreview":{"type":"code","value":"── 3-char suffixes that are NOT [X]+state ──\n# A tibble: 2 × 3\n  suffix3     n detention_facility                \n  <chr>   <int> <chr>                             \n1 OLD       171 ALBUQUERQUE HOLD ROOM             \n2 CDF         4 DENVER CONTRACT DETENTION FACILITY"},"elapsedMs":243}</script></tool-result></div>



<div><tool-call id="toolu_01XCHJE5wBTQgEE6YbNaAMxf"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"# ── Q2: name → multiple codes ─────────────────────────────────────────────────\nname_to_c...[truncated, total 461 chars]","intent":"Find detention_facility names that map to multiple codes"},"intent":"Find detention_facility names that map to multiple codes","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"# ── Q2: name → multiple codes ─────────────────────────────────────────────────\nname_to_codes <- codes |>\n  group_by(detention_facility, state) |>\n  summarise(\n    n_codes = n(),\n    codes   = paste(sort(detention_facility_code), collapse = \", \"),\n    .groups = \"drop\"\n  ) |>\n  filter(n_codes > 1) |>\n  arrange(desc(n_codes), detention_facility)\n\ncat(\"Facilities (name+state) with more than one code:\", nrow(name_to_codes), \"\\n\\n\")\nprint(name_to_codes, n = 30)","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-result call-id="toolu_01XCHJE5wBTQgEE6YbNaAMxf"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"status":"success","result":{"type":"content","value":[{"type":"text","text":"Output:\nFacilities (name+state) with more than one code: 0 \n\n# A tibble: 0 × 4\n# ℹ 4 varia...[truncated, total 185 chars]"}]},"resultPreview":{"type":"code","value":"Facilities (name+state) with more than one code: 0 \n\n# A tibble: 0 × 4\n# ℹ 4 variables: detention_facility <chr>, state <chr>, n_codes <int>, codes <chr>"},"elapsedMs":306}</script></tool-result></div>

Clean answers to both questions:

**Suffix structure:** Once you exclude plain state-abbreviation endings (which account for the vast majority of 7-char codes), only two meaningful type suffixes emerge: `HOLD` (171 holding rooms) and `CDF` (4 contract detention facilities). Everything else in the 7-char space is purely geographic — `[location abbreviation][state]`. The prefix patterns table from earlier was a red herring: `MON` appearing 6 times just means 6 different facilities whose location abbreviations begin with MON (Monroe County, Montgomery, etc.). There's no shared identity there.

**Multiple codes per facility:** The 853 codes vs. 841 names discrepancy is entirely explained by **generic county names appearing in multiple states** — Clinton County Jail in NY and IN, Jefferson County Jail in ID and IL, Washington County Jail in MN, UT, and AR, and so on. Every single duplicate is a different physical facility that happens to share a common name. The code-to-facility relationship is clean 1:1 once state is included.

The coding scheme is therefore: `[3–4 char location abbreviation][2-char state]`, with `HOLD` replacing the state suffix for holding rooms and `ICDF` replacing it for contract detention facilities. The BOP prefix (6-char codes) and the 3-char SPC/staging codes are entirely separate systems.


<span class="suggestion">Use the confirmed `[location][state]` structure to build a first-pass join against `canonical_facilities` by state, then fuzzy-match the location prefix against a cleaned version of `canonical_name`.</span>


<span class="suggestion">Create a `facility_type_ddp` classification column derived from code structure: `SPC/staging` (3-char), `BOP` (6-char BOP prefix), `HOLD` (HOLD suffix), `CDF` (CDF suffix), `IGSA` (all other 7-char) — and compare this taxonomy against `facility_type_wiki` in the panel.</span>

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01TDN8FjrjKHtfdvVkF23WB8"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"library(dplyr)\n\n# Canonical facilities active since FY23 (overlapping with DDP window Sep ...[truncated, total 1285 chars]","intent":"Check how many active canonical facilities have state-level coverage in DDP data"},"intent":"Check how many active canonical facilities have state-level coverage in DDP data","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"library(dplyr)\n\n# Canonical facilities active since FY23 (overlapping with DDP window Sep 2023+)\nactive_canonical <- facility_presence |>\n  filter(FY23 | FY24 | FY25 | FY26) |>\n  left_join(\n    canonical_facilities |> select(canonical_id, facility_state),\n    by = \"canonical_id\"\n  )\n\ncat(\"Canonical facilities active since FY23:\", nrow(active_canonical), \"\\n\")\ncat(\"States represented:\", n_distinct(active_canonical$facility_state), \"\\n\\n\")\n\n# DDP codes that could match official detention facilities\n# (non-HOLD 7-char + 3-char SPCs + 6-char BOP, excluding holding rooms)\ndp_official <- codes |>\n  filter(!str_ends(detention_facility_code, \"HOLD\")) |>\n  rename(facility_state = state)\n\ncat(\"DDP non-holding-room facilities:\", nrow(dp_official), \"\\n\")\ncat(\"States represented:\", n_distinct(dp_official$facility_state), \"\\n\\n\")\n\n# First-pass: how many active canonical facilities have at least one\n# DDP entry in the same state?\nstate_coverage <- active_canonical |>\n  mutate(state_in_ddp = facility_state %in% dp_official$facility_state)\n\ncat(\"Active canonical facilities with state present in DDP:\", \n    sum(state_coverage$state_in_ddp), \"/\", nrow(state_coverage), \"\\n\")\ncat(\"States not covered by DDP:\\n\")\nstate_coverage |> filter(!state_in_ddp) |> count(facility_state) |> print()","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-result call-id="toolu_01TDN8FjrjKHtfdvVkF23WB8"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"status":"success","result":{"type":"content","value":[{"type":"text","text":"Output:\nCanonical facilities active since FY23: 239 \nStates represented: 49 \n\nDDP non-hold...[truncated, total 344 chars]"}]},"resultPreview":{"type":"code","value":"Canonical facilities active since FY23: 239 \nStates represented: 49 \n\nDDP non-holding-room facilities: 682 \nStates represented: 54 \n\nActive canonical facilities with state present in DDP: 238 / 239 \nStates not covered by DDP:\n# A tibble: 1 × 2\n  facility_state     n\n  <chr>          <int>\n1 Cuba               1"},"elapsedMs":436}</script></tool-result></div>



<div><thinking id="thinking-1773287243737-jnuh2">
Great! 238 out of 239 active canonical facilities have their state represented in the DDP data. The single exception is the Guantanamo Bay facility in Cuba, which is expected.

Now let me do the name-based fuzzy matching. I need to:1. Extract the location prefix from DDP codes (first 5 chars for 7-char codes, first 3 chars for 3-char SPC codes, first 3 chars after BOP for 6-char codes)
2. Clean canonical names to extract a comparable abbreviation
3. Match by state + fuzzy name

For the DDP code prefix extraction:


- 7-char non-HOLD: prefix = first 5 chars, state = last 2 chars
- 7-char HOLD: skip (holding rooms)
- 7-char CDF: prefix = first 4 chars (e.g. DENI from DENICDF), state = not encoded
- 3-char SPC: the whole code
- 6-char BOP: prefix = last 3 chars of code (after BOP), or use facility name

For the canonical name cleaning, I should remove common suffixes like "Detention Center", "Correctional Center", "Jail", and "Facility" to get a comparable abbreviation for matching.

Rather than manually extracting an abbreviation, I'll fuzzy-match the DDP location prefix against the start of the cleaned canonical name using Jaro-Winkler distance, which works well for short strings like "STWRT" matching "STEWART" or "ADAMS" matching "ADAMS".

But looking at the actual patterns, some abbreviations seem to pull from the full facility name rather than just the location—like "MSVPC" coming from "Moshannon Valley Processing Center" or "STCDF" from "South Texas [something] Center"—so I need to account for that possibility too. I'm realizing that a simpler approach might work better—just clean up the canonical names by removing punctuation and spaces, then compare the first 5 characters of both the cleaned name and the DDP code prefix using string distance metrics to handle cases where exact matching fails. Looking at the actual data, I see the prefix matching works well for some cases like STWRT matching STEWART, but fails for acronyms like MSVPC for Moshannon Valley Processing Center. I'll start with the Jaro-Winkler approach on the prefix and first word, then add acronym matching as a fallback if needed. I need to step back and think about this more systematically. Rather than trying to decode each facility code individually, let me work from the canonical facility names themselves. I'll normalize each name to uppercase, extract the first meaningful word, and then compare that against the DDP code prefixes within the same state to find matches based on string similarity. Building the similarity matrix...
</thinking></div>

Good — 238/239 match by state (only Cuba/Guantanamo doesn't, as expected). Now let me do the name matching.

<div><tool-call id="toolu_0188RznLLrDUB1d482bDXXwU"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"library(stringdist)\n\n# ── Prep canonical side ────────────────────────────────────────────...[truncated, total 2673 chars]","intent":"Fuzzy-match canonical facility names against DDP code prefixes within state"},"intent":"Fuzzy-match canonical facility names against DDP code prefixes within state","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"library(stringdist)\n\n# ── Prep canonical side ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n# Extract first significant word (skip directional/generic openers)\nskip_words <- c(\"^NORTH \", \"^SOUTH \", \"^EAST \", \"^WEST \", \"^CENTRAL \",\n                \"^SOUTHWEST \", \"^SOUTHEAST \", \"^NORTHWEST \", \"^NORTHEAST \",\n                \"^NEW \", \"^PORT \", \"^FORT \", \"^CAMP \")\n\nclean_first_word <- function(name) {\n  s <- toupper(name)\n  # Strip leading directional/generic words to get the most distinctive token\n  for (pat in skip_words) s <- str_remove(s, pat)\n  # Take first word, remove non-alpha\n  str_extract(s, \"^[A-Z]+\")\n}\n\ncanonical_active <- active_canonical |>\n  left_join(canonical_facilities |> select(canonical_id, canonical_name),\n            by = \"canonical_id\") |>\n  filter(facility_state != \"Cuba\") |>\n  mutate(\n    name_key = clean_first_word(canonical_name),\n    name_key5 = str_sub(name_key, 1, 5)\n  )\n\n# ── Prep DDP side ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n# For 7-char non-HOLD non-CDF codes: prefix = first 5, state = last 2\n# For 3-char SPC codes: prefix = code itself (no state suffix)\n# For 6-char BOP codes: prefix = chars 4-6 (after BOP)\ndp_for_match <- dp_official |>\n  mutate(\n    code_len = nchar(detention_facility_code),\n    code_prefix = case_when(\n      code_len == 7 & !str_ends(detention_facility_code, \"CDF\") ~\n        str_sub(detention_facility_code, 1, 5),\n      code_len == 6 & str_starts(detention_facility_code, \"BOP\") ~\n        str_sub(detention_facility_code, 4, 6),\n      code_len == 3 ~ detention_facility_code,\n      code_len == 7 & str_ends(detention_facility_code, \"CDF\") ~\n        str_sub(detention_facility_code, 1, 4),\n      TRUE ~ str_sub(detention_facility_code, 1, 5)\n    )\n  )\n\n# ── Match within state ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\nmatch_results <- canonical_active |>\n  left_join(dp_for_match, by = \"facility_state\", relationship = \"many-to-many\") |>\n  mutate(\n    sim = stringsim(name_key5, code_prefix, method = \"jw\")\n  ) |>\n  group_by(canonical_id, canonical_name, facility_state, name_key5) |>\n  slice_max(sim, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>\n  ungroup() |>\n  select(canonical_id, canonical_name, facility_state, name_key5,\n         detention_facility_code, detention_facility, code_prefix, sim) |>\n  arrange(desc(sim))\n\ncat(\"Match quality distribution:\\n\")\nmatch_results |>\n  mutate(quality = cut(sim,\n    breaks = c(0, 0.6, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.0),\n    labels = c(\"<0.60 (poor)\", \"0.60–0.75\", \"0.75–0.85\", \"0.85–0.95\", \"≥0.95 (strong)\"),\n    include.lowest = TRUE)) |>\n  count(quality) |>\n  print()\n\ncat(\"\\nTop 20 matches:\\n\")\nmatch_results |> head(20) |> print()","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><thinking id="thinking-1773287343332-9sxxv">
The results are very informative. Let me analyze:

- **97 strong matches (≥0.95)** - these are likely correct matches
- **37 good matches (0.85-0.95)** - probably mostly correct
- **16 moderate matches (0.75-0.85)** - need review
- **49 fair matches (0.60-0.75)** - many likely wrong
- **39 poor matches (<0.60)** - definitely wrong or not in DDP

Looking at the weak matches:
- "Chittenden Regional Correctional Facility" VT → "VTCHT" (code prefix) - interesting, this suggests the DDP code uses "VT" prefix (state) + "CHT" (abbreviation). My extraction of 5-char prefix + 2-char state fails here because the state code is embedded in the MIDDLE of the prefix for Vermont codes.


- "San Juan Staging" PR → "SJS" with sim 0.556 - the 3-char code actually matches correctly, but my similarity score is low because I'm comparing against "SAN" instead of "JUAN". I need to handle skip_words better for shorter codes.
- "Kansas City Holdroom" MO is showing up as a canonical facility, which is odd - this might be bleeding in from the ICE data.
- "Two Bridges Regional Jail" ME is another case I should examine.

Looking at the pattern here, I'm seeing that many of these "weak" matches are actually correct — the string similarity metric just breaks down when the DDP code uses state prefixes, acronyms like TBRJL for Two Bridges Regional Jail, or pulls from a different part of the facility name. The 97 strong matches are almost certainly right, and some canonical facilities genuinely might not exist in the DDP dataset.

For the 39 poorest matches, I need to dig deeper to separate actual naming convention mismatches from facilities that are truly absent. The Kansas City Holdroom and similar entries are intriguing — they could be ICE tracking entries for temporary holding spaces rather than permanent facilities. Let me focus on understanding what those 97 strong matches look like and whether the poor matches follow a consistent pattern that would explain them away.

Looking at the Chittenden case specifically, the code structure is puzzling me. The canonical name maps to "CHITT" but the DDP code prefix is "VTCHT" — that's state abbreviation plus a three-letter facility abbreviation. If the full code is seven characters, I need to figure out what the complete code actually is and whether there's a redundant pattern like [state][abbreviation][state] being used.

Let me step back from the specifics here. The real issue is that DDP codes don't follow a consistent pattern across all facilities. Some use one structure, others use different ones, which means my simple prefix-extraction approach isn't reliable for every code. Given how thoroughly I've analyzed this, I should wrap up and present the 97 strong matches I've identified.

For the broader question about whether these facilities are in DDP, I can confirm about 97 with high confidence, another 50 or so are likely candidates, bringing the total to roughly 150 out of 238. The remaining 88 aren't necessarily missing from DDP—my matching strategy just isn't catching them. This is exploratory work, and I've made enough tool calls to present findings and suggest what to investigate next.
</thinking></div>



The preliminary picture is clear. Of 238 active canonical facilities (excluding Cuba/Guantanamo):

| Match quality | Count | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| ≥ 0.95 (strong) | 97 | Almost certainly correct matches |
| 0.85 – 0.95 | 37 | Probably correct |
| 0.75 – 0.85 | 16 | Plausible, needs review |
| 0.60 – 0.75 | 49 | Uncertain |
| < 0.60 (poor) | 39 | Wrong best-match or facility absent from DDP |

The weak tail reveals two distinct problems:

**1. The naming strategy breaks for certain code patterns.** The [location prefix][state] assumption isn't universal — some DDP codes embed the state at the *start* rather than the end (e.g., `VTCHT__` for the Vermont Chittenden facility). Others use true acronyms (`TBRJL` = Two Bridges Regional Jail) where the first word "TWO" has no similarity to "TBRJL". These aren't missing from DDP — they're just poorly matched by this strategy.

**2. Some genuinely may not be in DDP.** A few like "Cimmarron Correctional Facility" (OK) match only a completely unrelated facility, suggesting it may not appear in the daily population data at all — possibly because it was active in the ICE spreadsheets but had no individual-level detention records in the FOIA-released data.

Also notable: "Kansas City Holdroom" and "San Juan Staging" appear in our `canonical_facilities` — they're in the official ICE spreadsheets as named entries, not just in the DDP data. That's worth knowing for the crosswalk.

I'll suggest some questions below.


<span class="suggestion">Examine the 39 poor-match canonical facilities in detail — for each, search the full DDP name field (not just prefix) for substring matches against the canonical name, to separate "present but poorly coded" from "genuinely absent."</span>


<span class="suggestion">Check whether the strong-match pairs are actually correct by spot-inspecting 10–15 of them: confirm that `detention_facility` names align with `canonical_name` semantically, not just by prefix similarity.</span>


<span class="suggestion">Build the crosswalk in two tiers: high-confidence matches (sim ≥ 0.95) as automatic links, everything else as candidates requiring either a better matching strategy or manual resolution.</span>

https://www.prisonlegalnews.org/media/publications/ICE_DMCP_Facility_List_ERO_Custody_Management_Division_2017.pdf

<div><tool-result call-id="toolu_01A9sgMTfa5yCHF4LqNUAKSL"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"status":"success","result":{"type":"text","value":"[File: 7.5KB, 143 lines, showing lines 1-143]\n     1→# ice-detention project memory\n     2...[truncated, total 8706 chars]"},"resultPreview":{"type":"code","value":"[File: 7.5KB, 143 lines, showing lines 1-143]\n# ice-detention project memory\n\n## Related projects\n\nThe **wiki-graph** project at `~/Dropbox (Personal)/R/wiki-graph/` is the original source for `import-ice-detention.qmd`, from which this pipeline was extracted and refactored. Agents should feel free to read files in that folder for reference (e.g., original Qmd code, `AGENTS.md`, conversation plans).\n\n## Project overview\n\n`targets`-based R pipeline for importing, cleaning, and harmonizing 8 years of U.S. ICE detention facility statistics (FY 2019–FY 2026). Extracted and refactored from `import-ice-detention.qmd` in the `wiki-graph` project.\n\n## File system conventions\n\n- Project root: `~/Dropbox (Personal)/R/ice-detention/`\n- Raw data and derived outputs in `data/`\n- All pipeline functions in `R/` (auto-sourced by `tar_source()`)\n- Use `here::here()` for paths in scripts outside `_targets.R`\n\n## Data sources\n\nAll raw spreadsheets live in `data/`:\n\n| File | Fiscal Year |\n|------|-------------|\n| `FY19-detentionstats.xlsx` | FY 2019 |\n| `FY20-detentionstats.xlsx` | FY 2020 |\n| `FY21-detentionstats.xlsx` | FY 2021 |\n| `FY22-detentionStats.xlsx` | FY 2022 |\n| `FY23_detentionStats.xlsx` | FY 2023 |\n| `FY24_detentionStats.xlsx` | FY 2024 |\n| `FY25_detentionStats09242025.xlsx` | FY 2025 |\n| `FY26_detentionStats02022026.xlsx` | FY 2026 |\n\nSource URL pattern: `https://www.ice.gov/doclib/detention/FY{nn}-detentionstats.xlsx`\n\nEach file has a \"Facilities FY{nn}\" sheet with a 2-row merged header. Header row positions and rightmost data column vary by year — all encoded in `build_data_file_info()`.\n\n## Derived data outputs (in `data/`)\n\n| File | Description |\n|------|-------------|\n| `facility_crosswalk.rds/.csv` | Maps every (name, city, state) variant to `canonical_id` / `canonical_name` |\n| `facility_presence.rds/.csv` | One row per canonical facility; FY19–FY26 booleans + trajectory classification |\n| `facilities_panel.rds/.csv` | Long panel: one row per facility per year, all ~30 measurement variables |\n| `facilities_fy26_wiki_matches.rds` | FY26 rows with Wikipedia slug and management columns added |\n| `contractors-patch-fy26.csv` | Manual management company corrections for 98 FY26 facilities |\n| `ice-detention-data-dictionary-FY26.md/.csv` | Variable definitions and acronym guide |\n\n## `_targets.R` pipeline targets\n\n| Target | Function | Description |\n|--------|----------|-------------|\n| `data_file_info` | `build_data_file_info()` | Tibble of file paths, sheet names, header row positions |\n| `raw_xlsx_files` | `download_ice_spreadsheets()` | Downloads missing files (cue = \"never\") |\n| `clean_names_list` | `build_clean_names()` | Named list of variable name vectors, one per year |\n| `facilities_raw` | `import_all_years()` | Named list of raw tibbles, one per year |\n| `facilities_clean` | `clean_all_years()` | Name/city standardization, type coercion, Guantanamo patch |\n| `facilities_aggregated` | `aggregate_all_years()` | Row-level ADP sums, `share_non_crim`, `share_no_threat`, `facility_type_wiki` |\n| `facility_crosswalk` | `build_facility_crosswalk()` | Exact + fuzzy address matching → canonical IDs |\n| `facilities_keyed` | `attach_canonical_ids()` | Panel data with `canonical_id` / `canonical_name` joined |\n| `facility_presence` | `build_facility_presence()` | Per-facility year presence + trajectory label |\n| `facilities_panel` | `build_panel()` | Long format panel dataset |\n| `saved_files` | `save_outputs()` | Writes RDS + CSV to `data/`; format = \"file\" |\n\n## R/ function files\n\n| File | Key functions |\n|------|---------------|\n| `metadata.R` | `build_data_file_info()` |\n| `download.R` | `download_ice_spreadsheets()` |\n| `import.R` | `header_rows()`, `clean_variable_names_from_header()`, `build_clean_names()`, `read_facilities_data()`, `import_all_years()` |\n| `clean.R` | `clean_facility_names()`, `fix_city_names()`, `clean_facilities_data()`, `clean_all_years()` |\n| `aggregate.R` | `aggregate_facilities_data()`, `aggregate_all_years()` |\n| `crosswalk.R` | `build_facility_crosswalk()`, `attach_canonical_ids()`, `build_facility_presence()`, `build_panel()`, `save_outputs()` |\n| `add-wikipedia-matches.R` | `add_wikipedia_matches(df, name_col, lang=\"en\")` — uses WikipediR / MediaWiki API, rate-limited |\n| `gh-copy-assets.R` | JS copy-to-clipboard helper for HTML output |\n\n## Key variables\n\nAll column names are snake_case derived from the 2-row Excel headers.\n\n| Variable | Description |\n|----------|-------------|\n| `facility_name`, `facility_city`, `facility_state`, `facility_zip`, `facility_address` | Facility identity |\n| `facility_type_detailed` | Raw ICE type code (IGSA, DIGSA, SPC, BOP, CDF, etc.) |\n| `facility_type_wiki` | Derived readable type for Wikipedia use |\n| `facility_average_length_of_stay_alos` | Average length of stay |\n| `adp_detainee_classification_level_{a-d}` | ADP by security level |\n| `adp_criminality_male_crim/non_crim`, `adp_criminality_female_crim/non_crim` | ADP by criminality × gender |\n| `adp_ice_threat_level_{1-3}`, `adp_no_ice_threat_level` | ADP by ICE threat level |\n| `adp_mandatory` | Mandatory detention ADP |\n| `inspections_guaranteed_minimum` | Contracted bed capacity (integer) |\n| `inspections_last_inspection_type`, `..._end_date`, `..._standard`, `..._final_rating` | Inspection info |\n| `share_non_crim`, `share_no_threat` | Derived: non-criminal / no-threat shares |\n| `canonical_id`, `canonical_name` | Post-crosswalk facility identifiers |\n\n## Facility type → wiki classification\n\n| `facility_type_detailed` contains | `facility_type_wiki` |\n|-----------------------------------|----------------------|\n| IGSA | Jail |\n| USMS IGA | Jail |\n| DIGSA | Dedicated Migrant Detention Center |\n| STATE | State Migrant Detention Center |\n| BOP | Federal Prison |\n| Family | Private Family Detention Center |\n| CDF / USMS CDF | Private Migrant Detention Center |\n| SPC | ICE Migrant Detention Center |\n| STAGING | ICE Short-Term Migrant Detention Center |\n| DOD | Military Detention Center |\n\n## Crosswalk logic\n\n**Pass 1:** Exact match on `(facility_name, facility_city, facility_state)` → provisional `facility_id`.\n\n**Pass 2:** Within the same ZIP code, compute OSA string similarity on `facility_address`. Pairs with similarity ≥ 0.80 are merged via union-find, except for pairs in `do_not_merge_pairs()`.\n\n**Canonical name:** Most recent year's name for each cluster (ties broken by `year_order`).\n\n**Manual overrides:** `canonical_name_overrides_table()` in `crosswalk.R` — 7 entries correcting ambiguous state suffixes, punctuation, etc.\n\n**Trajectory labels** (in `facility_presence`):\n- `continuous` — present every year FY19–FY26\n- `persistent_gaps` — present in FY19 and FY26 but with gaps\n- `closed` — present in FY19, absent from FY26\n- `new` — absent from FY19, present in FY26\n- `transient` — absent from both FY19 and FY26\n\n## What is NOT yet ported from the original Qmd\n\nThe following sections of `import-ice-detention.qmd` (wiki-graph) have not yet been added as targets:\n\n1. **Wikipedia harmonization** — fuzzy matching of ICE facilities to the Wikipedia \"List of immigrant detention sites\" article; adds `wiki_slug` and `management` columns. Uses `add_wikipedia_matches()` already in `R/`.\n2. **Wikitext table generation** — `get_wikitable()` converts the panel to MediaWiki markup.\n3. **Visualizations** — treemaps (by facility type; by state × type) and three interactive Leaflet maps.\n\n## Dependencies\n\nCore: `readxl`, `dplyr`, `stringr`, `tidyr`, `purrr`, `tibble`, `httr`, `stringdist`, `glue`, `targets`\n\nFor Wikipedia targets (not yet added): `WikipediR`, `rvest`\n\nFor visualization targets (not yet added): `treemapify`, `ggplot2`, `mapview`, `leaflet`\n"},"elapsedMs":88}</script></tool-result></div>

**`pdftools` is not installed anywhere on this device** — neither in the renv environment nor in the system/user libraries. It also requires `poppler`, a system library for PDF rendering. Here's how to install both:

**1. Install the `poppler` system library** (if you haven't already — required by `pdftools`):

```bash
# macOS via Homebrew
brew install poppler
```

**2. Install `pdftools` into the renv environment:**

```r
renv::install("pdftools")
```

**3. Snapshot the dependency:**

```r
renv::snapshot()
```

What were you looking to use `pdftools` for? Happy to continue from there.

Now I have enough to write a full parser. Each record spans 2 lines (main row + DETLOC 1px below); the column boundaries come from the header x-positions. Let me build it.

<div><tool-call id="toolu_01AxmmRiy8Jgf6cfdSUkFcum"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"library(pdftools)\nlibrary(dplyr)\nlibrary(tibble)\nlibrary(purrr)\n\n# Column x-position break...[truncated, total 2259 chars]","intent":"Build and run the PDF column parser"},"intent":"Build and run the PDF column parser","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"library(pdftools)\nlibrary(dplyr)\nlibrary(tibble)\nlibrary(purrr)\n\n# Column x-position breakpoints derived from the header at y=63 on page 1\ncol_breaks <- c(\n  0, 34, 101, 151, 179, 206, 218, 228, 242, 260, 282, 298, 317,\n  337, 349, 361, 373, 385, 397, 409, 422, 434, 469, 483, 510, 536,\n  622, 642, 669, 687, 706, 734, 754, 782, 811, 840, 869, 898, 920, 944,\n  Inf\n)\n\ncol_names <- c(\n  \"detloc\", \"name\", \"address\", \"city\", \"county\", \"state\", \"zip\",\n  \"type\", \"type_detailed\", \"male_female\", \"levels\",\n  \"capacity\", \"population_count\",\n  \"fy17_adp\", \"fy16_adp\", \"fy15_adp\", \"fy14_adp\", \"fy13_adp\",\n  \"fy12_adp\", \"fy11_adp\", \"fy10_adp\",\n  \"facility_operator\", \"facility_owner\",\n  \"contract_initiation_date\", \"contract_expiration_date\",\n  \"per_diem_rate_detailed\", \"authorizing_authority\",\n  \"over_under_72\", \"last_inspection_type\", \"last_inspection_standard\",\n  \"last_inspection_rating_final\", \"last_inspection_date\",\n  \"cy16_rating\", \"cy15_rating\", \"cy14_rating\", \"cy13_rating\",\n  \"cy12_rating\", \"cy11_rating\", \"cy10_rating\", \"dsm_assigned\"\n)\n\n# Assign column to each token by x-position\nassign_col <- function(x_val) {\n  idx <- findInterval(x_val, col_breaks, rightmost.closed = FALSE)\n  col_names[idx]\n}\n\n# Parse a single page: data_y_min is the first y with actual data\nparse_page <- function(page_tokens, data_y_min) {\n  toks <- page_tokens |>\n    filter(y >= data_y_min) |>\n    arrange(y, x) |>\n    mutate(col = assign_col(x))\n\n  if (nrow(toks) == 0) return(tibble())\n\n  # Group into records: new group when gap between consecutive y > 1\n  y_vals <- toks$y\n  gaps <- c(TRUE, diff(y_vals) > 1)\n  toks <- toks |> mutate(record_id = cumsum(gaps))\n\n  # Within each record, collapse tokens per column\n  toks |>\n    group_by(record_id, col) |>\n    summarise(value = paste(text, collapse = \" \"), .groups = \"drop\") |>\n    tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = col, values_from = value, values_fill = NA) |>\n    select(-record_id)\n}\n\n# Page 1 data starts at y=66\npage1 <- parse_page(pdf_dat[[1]], data_y_min = 66)\n\n# Pages 2-4 data starts at top (y=29 on page 2)\npages_rest <- map(pdf_dat[2:4], parse_page, data_y_min = 1)\n\npdf_2017_raw <- bind_rows(page1, pages_rest)\n\ncat(\"Rows:\", nrow(pdf_2017_raw), \"\\n\")\ncat(\"Cols:\", ncol(pdf_2017_raw), \"\\n\")\nglimpse(pdf_2017_raw)\n","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

271 rows, all 40 columns — looking solid. One thing to fix: the `facility_operator` for Adelanto shows `"0 GEO"` because its FY10 ADP of `0` lands at x=435, just past the boundary I set. Let me tighten the break.

<div><thinking id="thinking-1774109820217-3av7k">
There are 10 rows with this issue. The FY10 ADP "0" is being split - some rows have it in fy10_adp and facility_operator (showing "0 GEO"), and some rows have it only in facility_operator (showing "0 GEO" with fy10_adp being NA). The "0" in facility_operator needs to be stripped, and the actual operator name needs to remain.

The fix is:
1. Adjust the col_breaks to use 437 instead of 434 for the facility_operator start
2. OR just clean up the facility_operator column by:
   - If facility_operator starts with digits, move those digits to fy10_adp and keep the rest as facility_operator

I think adjusting the boundary is the cleaner approach. The "0" values are appearing at x=435, just past the old 434 boundary, so shifting it to 437 should capture them in the FY10 ADP column instead. Let me test this with the corrected boundary.
</thinking></div>



<div><tool-call id="toolu_011hXLJVbt1Uwt9skWnKHmwj"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"# Fix: push facility_operator boundary from 434 to 437 to capture stray \"0\" FY10 ADP value...[truncated, total 607 chars]","intent":"Reparse with corrected column boundary for FY10 ADP vs facility_operator"},"intent":"Reparse with corrected column boundary for FY10 ADP vs facility_operator","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"# Fix: push facility_operator boundary from 434 to 437 to capture stray \"0\" FY10 ADP values\ncol_breaks <- c(\n  0, 34, 101, 151, 179, 206, 218, 228, 242, 260, 282, 298, 317,\n  337, 349, 361, 373, 385, 397, 409, 422, 437, 469, 483, 510, 536,\n  622, 642, 669, 687, 706, 734, 754, 782, 811, 840, 869, 898, 920, 944,\n  Inf\n)\n\npage1 <- parse_page(pdf_dat[[1]], data_y_min = 66)\npages_rest <- map(pdf_dat[2:4], parse_page, data_y_min = 1)\npdf_2017_raw <- bind_rows(page1, pages_rest)\n\n# Verify fix\npdf_2017_raw |>\n  filter(grepl(\"^[0-9]\", facility_operator)) |>\n  select(detloc, name, fy10_adp, facility_operator)\n","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>

<div><tool-result call-id="toolu_011hXLJVbt1Uwt9skWnKHmwj"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"status":"success","result":{"type":"content","value":[{"type":"text","text":"Output:\n# A tibble: 0 × 4\n# ℹ 4 variables: detloc <chr>, name <chr>, fy10_adp <chr>, facil...[truncated, total 132 chars]"}]},"resultPreview":{"type":"code","value":"# A tibble: 0 × 4\n# ℹ 4 variables: detloc <chr>, name <chr>, fy10_adp <chr>, facility_operator <chr>"},"elapsedMs":960}</script></tool-result></div>

<div><tool-call id="toolu_012hMYrWaYgkNGeGsj4A2Bqe"><script type="application/json">{"version":1,"name":"runCode","input":{"code":"pdf_2017 <- pdf_2017_raw |>\n  filter(grepl(\"^[A-Z0-9]+$\", detloc)) |>\n  # Reorder to match...[truncated, total 982 chars]","intent":"Filter to valid DETLOC rows only and preview final parsed data frame"},"intent":"Filter to valid DETLOC rows only and preview final parsed data frame","inputPreview":{"type":"code","value":"pdf_2017 <- pdf_2017_raw |>\n  filter(grepl(\"^[A-Z0-9]+$\", detloc)) |>\n  # Reorder to match 2015 XLSX column order\n  select(detloc, name, address, city, county, state, zip,\n         type, type_detailed, male_female, levels, capacity,\n         population_count, fy17_adp, fy16_adp, fy15_adp, fy14_adp,\n         fy13_adp, fy12_adp, fy11_adp, fy10_adp,\n         facility_operator, facility_owner,\n         contract_initiation_date, contract_expiration_date,\n         per_diem_rate_detailed, authorizing_authority, over_under_72,\n         last_inspection_type, last_inspection_standard,\n         last_inspection_rating_final, last_inspection_date,\n         cy16_rating, cy15_rating, cy14_rating, cy13_rating,\n         cy12_rating, cy11_rating, cy10_rating, dsm_assigned)\n\ncat(\"Final rows:\", nrow(pdf_2017), \"\\n\")\ncat(\"Any remaining duplicates:\", anyDuplicated(pdf_2017$detloc), \"\\n\\n\")\n\npdf_2017 |> select(detloc, name, city, state, type_detailed, capacity, fy17_adp, facility_operator)\n","language":"r"}}</script></tool-call></div>
